## [03:03 - 06:03]

is another one of those. Go after the stuff that is most likely to be profitable. Now if we're going to talk about keywords, we need to talk about this other term, search terms. I've known people that have run AdWords accounts for years before they really made the distinction between these two things. So let's figure it out early. The keyword is what you choose. The keyword is what lives inside your AdWords account and it acts like a magnet and it attracts search terms that are similar to the keyword. The search term is what the person, the searcher, actually goes and types into Google. So for example, if I had typed into Google Samsung 4K TV and you had the keyword Samsung TV in your AdWords account, then there's a pretty decent match between those two. We'll worry about different types of matching later on, but essentially those two match and so there's a good chance that I'm going to see your ad. Same with TV from Samsung or the slightly misspelled Samsung TV. All of those three are a pretty close match to Samsung TV and there's a good chance that I'll see your ad. In certain circumstances I might, but let's not worry about those now. I probably won't see your ad. ## [09:06 - 12:11]

spend an hour playing with that, and learn how to use that, you'll be golden. Now, if we're going to talk keywords, we need to talk about this thing called keyword match types. This is something that confuses a lot of people that are new to Google AdWords, and it really doesn't need to. Once you understand the meanings of these three words in front of you, exact phrase and broad match, then it will all make complete sense. And two minutes from now, that's where you're going to be. So exact match first. The exact match keyword is denoted with these square brackets that you see right at the top of the screen. So if we choose the exact match keyword of Samsung TV, and we tell Google, I only want the exact match keyword Samsung TV, that tells Google only match to people that are typing in. Remember the difference in keywords and search queries, only match to the search query, which is exactly Samsung TV. If there are other words before or after Samsung TV, then it's not going to be a match. So Samsung 4K TV with a new word in the middle there, isn't going to be a match. Cheap Samsung TV isn't going to be a match. Only the exact search query is an exact match to that keyword. Exact match keywords. So exact match are very powerful in a way in that you don't really need to worry about making mistakes. You know exactly who you're going to get to your website if you use exact match keywords. The downside is it's a little bit limiting. So you need more and more keywords in order to get more people to your website in order to get more people seeing your ad. So the next level down if you like is phrase match keywords. Now phrase match is donated with the double quotes around the keyword that you see at the top there. So quote Samsung TV quote that says to Google, this is a phrase match keyword. I only want to match. I don't Google I only want you to match me to people that are searching for a search query that contains the phrase Samsung TV, which means these words have to be in that order with nothing in between them, but they can have other words before and or after. So Samsung TV is a match. Yes, it is exactly the same, but it also contains the phrase doesn't contain anything else, but it contains the phrase Samsung TV. And cheap Samsung TV also has the phrase Samsung TV in. So yes, that's a match. And then you read is eligible to show. Now Samsung 4K TV, obviously, is not a match for that phrase, because the phrase those two words in that order together with nothing in between them, the phrase Samsung TV does not appear in Samsung 4K TV, nor does it appear in this single word Samsung. And so the bottom two examples there, your ad would not show for. So this again, gets a little bit broader than exact match. So you don't have to have every single

## [12:11 - 15:16]

keyword in your account. But there are still some potentially relevant search queries that you're not going to show up for. And that's why some people will then run ads or let's say put keywords in the account that are broad match keywords. Now this is the default. If you don't understand match types, when you start typing keywords into your ad words account, this is what they're going to be. They're going to be broad. Now the danger of this is Google now has a lot of leeway to go and show your ads wherever it wants. So if you have the broad match keyword of Samsung TV, it will match all kinds of stuff Samsung TVs, cheap Samsung TV, possibly something like Samsung remote control, because Google's going to think, oh, that's kind of connected to Samsung TV. It's broadly related, hence broad match keyword, broadly related. Yeah, go on then. I'll show you your ad. And I'll charge you another couple of bucks. Thanks for coming. Now Samsung phone, hopefully it's not going to show for that because that's a little bit too far away from the original intent that we had here of Samsung TV. And what's meant to happen is that the words inside your keywords as Samsung and TV are meant to be inside the search query, but Google does allow a little bit of latitude here and sometimes quite a lot of latitude.
